turns.content: I've been reading up on how to set up my first fuzzer for a new open-source project, but I'm still stuck on the distinction between using afl::fuzz! and just running a standard test suite. It seems like the entry point is different from what I'd expect in normal code.

The key difference lies in how you structure your program's execution flow for fuzzing versus testing. When you use afl::fuzz!, you're essentially creating an infinite loop that runs until the process is killed or a specific condition is met, which differs from standard Rust main functions that return on completion. You need to wrap your instrumented code in a closure passed to afl::fuzz! and ensure it handles signals gracefully so the fuzzer can resume from where it left off without losing state.

That makes sense about the loop, but I'm confused about why we need instrumentation-based fuzzing specifically with this approach. Is that fundamentally different from how cargo-fuzz works or what ASan provides?

Instrumentation-based fuzzing with afl.rs is distinct because it relies on tracing memory accesses to determine which inputs are likely to hit edge cases, whereas cargo-fuzz uses a simpler generator model and ASan primarily detects memory safety violations like use-after-free. While ASan can catch crashes during execution, the AFL instrumentation tracks which code paths were executed, allowing you to focus your fuzzer on specific functions or logic blocks rather than relying solely on random input generation.

I see the distinction now, but I'm worried that setting up persistent mode in my Rust tooling is going to be a nightmare compared to using standard Cargo features. Is there really a significant overhead?

Persistent mode does require more setup than a simple Cargo build because you need to manage the fuzzer's state file and ensure your program can resume correctly after interruptions, but once configured, it provides better coverage without needing massive input generation. The trade-off is that you must handle signal safety explicitly in your Rust code using traits like std::process::SignalHandler and avoid unsafe blocks unless absolutely necessary, which adds some complexity compared to standard testing.

I've been trying to implement the persistent mode but my fuzzer keeps crashing with a segmentation fault when I try to resume. It seems like my current implementation isn't handling the state transitions properly. Can you walk me through how to ensure my Rust code is actually ready for this kind of fuzzing?

The crash usually happens because your program doesn't correctly handle the transition between normal execution and fuzzer control flow when signals are received. You need to ensure that any critical sections in your code are wrapped with proper synchronization primitives like std::sync::Mutex or Arc<T> if you're sharing state across threads, and verify that all external dependencies you've included support signal handling gracefully. Additionally, check that your main function correctly delegates control to the fuzzer entry point without attempting to perform I/O operations that might block unexpectedly.

I'm trying to port our legacy C project into a Rust service, but I hit a wall on fuzz testing. Our old tests rely heavily on instrumentation-based fuzzing where we patch memory access patterns, and I found that cargo-fuzz doesn't support this well. We need something like afl::fuzz! entry point logic, so I'm looking at the afl.rs crate to see if it can handle persistent mode for our stateful data structures without losing coverage.

The main reason cargo-fuzz struggles with your use case is that it wraps tests in a sandboxed environment where you cannot modify global memory or patch specific functions, which breaks the instrumentation-based approach. The afl.rs crate exposes an impl trait for fuzzing harnesses that allows you to write custom entry points similar to the C library's main loop but safely within Rust bounds. For persistent mode, you need to manage your state outside the fuzz function itself and pass it via arguments or closures so that coverage counters track real execution paths without needing to instrument every internal call.

That makes sense regarding the sandboxing limitation. I was hoping to avoid using ASan for triage since our project has complex memory layouts and we want to catch undefined behavior patterns that might be missed by static analysis alone. Can you explain how afl.rs handles the persistent state in persistent mode versus a simple one-shot test?

In persistent mode, afl.rs maintains an external database of inputs and outputs across runs so that successful seeds are reused while failed ones are discarded. You create a mutable struct to hold your application state outside the fuzz function, then pass it into each iteration via arguments or a closure context. This avoids needing to patch internal memory patterns because the framework manages the lifecycle of the input data. For ASan triage, you can combine both by running your Rust tests under Sanitizer and letting afl.rs report only those inputs that trigger Sanitizer errors.

I see how state management works now. But I'm still confused about how to actually write the fuzz harness function itself. Does afl.rs require me to implement a specific trait or just call a standard entry point?

You do need to implement a custom harness that conforms to the impl trait for fuzzing, which includes methods like setup and run. The framework expects your function to return an Option<Error> indicating success or failure. Once you define this interface, afl.rs will automatically inject coverage tracking before each call without requiring any manual instrumentation of internal functions.

Okay, so I don't need to patch memory access patterns anymore? That's huge for us since we were doing a lot of dynamic analysis in C. What about the comparison with cargo-fuzz then—does afl.rs offer any built-in mechanisms for comparing coverage metrics?

Afl.rs provides built-in support for generating and storing coverage reports directly into your project's output directory, which you can parse later with standard tools. While cargo-fuzz only exports basic test results without detailed coverage data, afl.rs integrates more deeply with the underlying fuzzing engine to give you granular path information.
That's helpful. I'm wondering if we can integrate this into our CI pipeline alongside ASan runs. Would there be any performance overhead from running afl.rs in addition to our existing test suite?

Yes, there is some overhead because afl.rs runs each fuzz iteration through the full instrumentation loop before passing control back to your harness. However, this cost is acceptable when you're triaging with ASan since you're already running extra tests anyway. You can tune thread counts in your configuration file to balance speed and coverage depth.
Thanks for clarifying the trade-offs. One last thing—does afl.rs support any kind of mutation strategies beyond random input generation?

Yes, you can implement custom mutation logic inside your harness function by calling helper methods exposed through the impl trait for fuzzing. This lets you apply deterministic changes to inputs based on previous test outcomes or external rules without needing to patch memory patterns directly.
